title: Lang-Trotter Phenomena and Unlikely Intersections
speaker: Georgios Papas
abstract:
I will discuss how the Lang–Trotter conjecture for pairs of elliptic curves implies new cases of the Zilber–Pink conjecture for curves in 3. Unlike previous results for curves in g, our result does not rely on any assumption on intersections with the boundary, and in particular applies to compact curves. The argument is based on the G-functions method of Yves André.
This is based on joint work in progress with Chris Daw.

proof_stages.label: Zilber-Pink target and conditional A_3 theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the curve case of Zilber-Pink, fixes the notion of a Hodge-generic curve, and states a conditional finiteness theorem for a curve in the moduli space A_3 whose base point lies on a split special surface E_1×E_2×E_3 with E_1 CM and E_2,E_3 non-CM over Q, assuming a Lang-Trotter conjecture for the pair E_2,E_3.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to a polynomial height bound
proof_stages.summary: The speaker compares earlier degeneration-at-the-boundary results with the present nondegenerate situation and formulates the needed theorem as a height bound of the form h(s) bounded polynomially in [K(s):K] for the relevant split-locus points on the curve.

proof_stages.label: G-functions and André’s height principle
proof_stages.summary: The proof tool is André’s G-function method: after defining G-functions, local analytic evaluation, and nontrivial algebraic relations among values, the speaker invokes a theorem bounding the height of algebraic input points once a global relation of controlled degree is produced.

proof_stages.label: Period G-functions and the close-place decomposition
proof_stages.summary: For the abelian-threefold family over the curve, a symplectic period matrix of G-functions is attached, and for each exceptional point s the places where s is close to the base point s_0 are divided into archimedean, ordinary-reduction, and supersingular-reduction classes.

proof_stages.label: Local factors and global algebraic relation
proof_stages.summary: At archimedean and supersingular places one builds bounded-degree local polynomial relations, while all ordinary places share a single bounded-degree relation; multiplying these local factors gives the required global relation, with degree controlled by the number of supersingular places.

proof_stages.label: Lang-Trotter control of supersingular places
proof_stages.summary: The pair Lang-Trotter conjecture for E_2,E_3 gives a logarithmic-logarithmic bound for primes at which both are supersingular, which bounds the supersingular contribution to the degree of the global relation and hence completes the polynomial height bound via the G-function theorem.

examples.name: Known curve cases in tori and abelian varieties
examples.note: The speaker cites products of algebraic tori and abelian varieties as established settings where the curve version of the unlikely-intersection statement is known, to situate the new A_3 result.

examples.name: The modular product Y(1)^n hitting the boundary
examples.note: Y(1)^n is contrasted with A_3 because curves there do meet the boundary, illustrating why prior degeneration-based methods do not cover the nondegenerate A_3 situation.
examples.engagement_reason: Y(1)^n is invoked only to say that curves there hit the boundary, without analyzing a particular curve or boundary intersection

passes.reason: the ordinary modular product Y(1)^n is used to show the analogous claim that curves need not hit the boundary fails there
boundary/cusp intersection is a limiting degeneration and is used to show the A_3 boundary-avoidance phenomenon fails for Y(1)^n
Y(1)^n is used to show that the boundary-avoidance phenomenon in A_3 does not extend there.
The ordinary modular product Y(1)^n is invoked to show that the A_3 boundary-avoidance phenomenon does not generalize there.
The boundary of Y(1)^n consists of limiting cusp degenerations, and it is invoked to show the analogous boundary-avoidance phenomenon is not true there.
Y(1)^n is used to show the boundary-hitting behavior differs from A_3, since every relevant curve meets the boundary there
Y(1)^n is used to show the non-boundary-hitting phenomenon from A_3 does not persist, and the boundary involved is cuspidal/degenerate.
The product Y(1)^n is used to show the analogous possibility of projective curves avoiding the boundary fails there, since closures of curves meet the boundary.
Used to show the non-boundary phenomenon fails, and the modular-product boundary consists of limiting degenerate cusp strata.
Used to show the A3 possibility of curves avoiding the boundary fails for Y(1)^n, whose cusp boundary is a limiting degeneration.
Used as the contrary modular-product case where the boundary-avoidance issue fails, and Y(1)^n is a highly regular modular product.
Y(1)^n is used to show the boundary-avoidance phenomenon in A_3 does not generalize, while the product itself is a standard modular family.

examples.name: Single-elliptic-curve trace-zero Lang-Trotter
examples.note: The classical case counting primes with a_p(E)=0 for one non-CM elliptic curve is recalled to explain how the pair version is a stronger simultaneous-supersingularity input.
examples.engagement_reason: the single non-CM elliptic-curve trace-zero Lang-Trotter case is recalled via its conjectural asymptotic and known bounds, but not worked through for a concrete curve

passes.reason: a single non-CM elliptic curve with trace-zero primes is presented as the standard special case of Lang-Trotter, not as a structural exception or refutation
classical one-curve non-CM Lang--Trotter fixed-trace-zero case, used as comparison rather than refutation

a fixed non-CM elliptic curve with trace-zero primes is the basic Lang--Trotter case, not a sporadic or extremal object
The trace-zero case counts supersingular reductions, which are extremal among elliptic reductions by having minimal p-rank and unusually large endomorphisms.
The trace-zero Lang--Trotter problem for one non-CM elliptic curve is presented as the minimal one-curve special case rather than as a counterexample.

examples.name: Many independent elliptic factors thought experiment
examples.note: The hypothetical use of many elliptic curves or a high-dimensional abelian variety illustrates how stronger independence of supersingular-prime conditions might compensate for weaker unconditional bounds.
